mission.title: Install and Configure Pixie on Kubernetes
mission.description: Pixie is an open-source observability tool for Kubernetes applications that provides instant insights into your cluster's performance and resource usage. Installing Pixie allows you to leverage its auto-telemetry and in-cluster edge compute capabilities for enhanced observability.

steps.title: Add the Pixie Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the Pixie Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add pixie https://artifacts.px.dev/helm_charts/operator
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Pixie using Helm
steps.description:
Install Pixie in a new namespace called 'pixie' with the following command:
```bash
helm install pixie pixie/pixie --namespace pixie --create-namespace --version release/cloud/v0.1.9
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Pixie pods are running successfully with the following command:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace pixie
```

steps.title: Access the Pixie UI
steps.description:
Port-forward the Pixie service to access the UI locally:
```bash
kubectl port-forward service/pixie --namespace pixie 8080:80
```
You can now access the Pixie UI at http://localhost:8080.

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Pixie will have all pods running in the 'pixie' namespace, and you will be able to access the Pixie UI through port forwarding. Additionally, resource limits will be set for optimal performance.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add pixie https://artifacts.px.dev/helm_charts/operator
helm repo update
helm install pixie pixie/pixie --namespace pixie --create-namespace --version release/cloud/v0.1.9

resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Pixie release from the cluster using Helm with the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall pixie --namespace pixie
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) associated with Pixie. Run the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd $(kubectl get crd | grep pixie | awk '{print $1}')
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace pixie
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the Pixie namespace and verify that it has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace pixie
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current state of the Pixie installation. You can do this by exporting the current configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace pixie -o yaml > pixie-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the Pixie installation to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade pixie pixie/pixie --namespace pixie --version release/cloud/v0.1.10
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the Pixie pods are running successfully after the upgrade:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace pixie
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If Pixie pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for any errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace pixie
```
Look for any error messages that might indicate the cause of the crash.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Pixie UI, verify that the service is running:
```bash
kubectl get services --namespace pixie
```
Ensure the service is correctly configured and the port-forward command is running.

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If Pixie is not starting due to resource constraints, check the resource usage:
```bash
kubectl top pods --namespace pixie
```
Adjust the resource limits in the deployment if necessary.

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails
troubleshooting.description:
If the Helm upgrade command fails, check for any existing Helm releases and their status:
```bash
helm list --namespace pixie
```
You may need to resolve any conflicts or issues before retrying the upgrade.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher and have Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
